item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked the skip!

The discussion of pictures in comments led me to the wiki page at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Huaxi_Village , which now does have an image, including the hotel, under CC-BY-SA 2.0, which, I hope, might allow its use in the SCP article.

It also seems from Wikipedia that SCP-2788 is a close reference to a real person? Or am I reading too much in here?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: As neither of these images are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0), they have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Scorpion451 here. This is a solid piece with lots of heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… he considered containment an option, 'cause at least a small part of him counted it as a good idea… that part being the agent they made part of the will.
And he stays contained 'cause the Foundation put MORE agents with that will in there~!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An absolutely astounding work. Incorporating the worldwide scope of the Foundation and including the best efforts of the org to humanize and collaborate are always ways to earn my admiration. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little necro here, but I like how 艹他祖宗十八代 is translated over here.

item.title: Late +100
item.content:encoded:
As of this post, the rating of the page is +111 (though one account is deleted).

It still has no downvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, as of this post, you're at +83 with zero downvotes.

This is a fantastic, charming article, so the praise is well-deserved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't so serious. It just is a hypocrisy of government image.
….Well I dare say there are something creepier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fantastic, clever piece with a solid emotional core. Plus, it's always nice to see the Foundation not acting like total psychopaths for once — they do right by the guy, and I like the way they work with him to make his containment more like a retirement.

item.title: Re: @Univine
item.content:encoded: Thanks, Eta Carinae! I'm really flattered by the positive response I've received for this SCP, it's far beyond what I expected.

item.title: @Univine
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, much better and clearer. THANKS!

ETA: WOW, +62! Well done, Univine!

item.title: Re: Lol.
item.content:encoded: I like this comment. ALL people are special, Rand42! ;>

item.title: Lol.
item.content:encoded: I'm Chinese and I got all the reference. Nice skip now I feel special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see why that would be the cause of some confusion. I've edited the relevant parts of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, OK. But I still think it's not clear, and particularly this sentence:

The Longxi International Hotel was modified to suit the purposes of containing SCP-2788, and designated Provisional Humanoid Containment Site-888.

(at the end of ITW2) is misleading: it does sound like the whole hotel was requisitioned! How about 'a few rooms of', or 'a small part of', something like that, at the beginning?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I'm not sure why you deemed it necessary to black box the year of discovery. Black boxing that information doesn't really seem to serve a purpose from my understanding, and appears like its black boxing for the sake of black boxing.

Yeah, that's a fair point, and I've put in an actual year.

2) The agent seems really quick to reveal the SCP foundation's existence to 2788. I realize that doing so was a rather simple way of gaining the cooperation of 2788, but still, so much for being a secret organization if the secret is revealed at the drop of a hat.

There's a couple of reasons why I think this makes sense in context:

1) In interview one, it's already been established that SCP-2788 knows that it's being watched, so it's already pretty suspicious of Agent Cheng's motives. Establishing SCP-2788's trust at this point is pretty crucial if he wants to contain it diplomatically.

2) In context, Agent Cheng only describes the Foundation's motives, which SCP-2788 will ultimately end up discovering regardless of how it enters containment. He doesn't mention any operational details, which I think are the more secret aspects of the Foundation.

3) Since it's already been established that SCP-2788 can't interact with people who aren't from Huaxi Village, the worst-case scenario is that SCP-2788 tries to tell the villagers about the Foundation and a bunch of people have to be administered amnestics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is what I get for not reading the containment procedures. D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Re: 2), if 2788 is affected by the whims and wishes of those around him, wouldn't telling him about the Foundation's wishes make him easier to contain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was neat. I personally was pulled into this by the middle of the containment procedures, which is something that doesn't happen very often for me, so kudos there. I also enjoyed the interviews, as I found the personality of 2788 to be quite likable, and tragic once you consider its completely at the whims of those around him.

If I was to nitpick, there would be two nits in particular I would pick:

1) I'm not sure why you deemed it necessary to black box the year of discovery. Black boxing that information doesn't really seem to serve a purpose from my understanding, and appears like its black boxing for the sake of black boxing.

2) The agent seems really quick to reveal the SCP foundation's existence to 2788. I realize that doing so was a rather simple way of gaining the cooperation of 2788, but still, so much for being a secret organization if the secret is revealed at the drop of a hat.

Overall +1. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The hotel is supposed to be containment for SCP-2788 only. Obviously, the Foundation had to keep him separate from the villagers for his containment, but they agreed to keep SCP-2788 somewhere central to the village, and where better than the massive, under-utilized skyscraper? It's not like they're using the entire thing to contain him, since they can just keep him in some restricted access area of the hotel. Besides, I'd imagine that SCP-2788 would prefer to live out the rest of his life in the comfort of a hotel than in the basement of some random house in the outskirts of the village.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I took a bit of artistic license here with the actual history of Huaxi Village, but it is a real (and in my opinion, rather creepy) place. There are a couple of articles written by visitors of Huaxi, which might interest you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A very good article, but there's something that is not clear to me:
Is the hotel containment for SCP-2788 only, or for the whole population of Huaxi?
If only for the SCP, why so huge a containment?
If for everyone… I feel sorry for them, going from quaint little houses with gardens to a huge skyscraper…! ;>
(In fact I feel sorry for SCP-2788 too. Why contain him apart from his villagers?)

Still, an interesting take on 'cult of personality', and on the passage from traditional to modern thinking/architecture. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heartily upvoted. A rare humanoid that is all around awesome to read. The only thing I would have liked to see is a little more insight as to how this being came to be in the 1300s, but I suppose that is part of the mystique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welp,
it was nice know'n you guys

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting. There's still something funky about the page image that sets off my digital artist senses. Might be they used a weird camera/lens like a large aperture telephoto or something to get that hyperfocus effect.

edit: also, looking at it zoomed in it somebody ran one hell of an unsharp filter over that image. That explains the hypersaturation and the "something is weird with the trees" vibe I was getting. Think its legit though, unless somebody sat there for a couple hours getting the overlap with the trees right on a CGI overlay just for a simple planning mockup image. (not likely)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huaxi Village is known as the "Number One Village Under The Sky". It was founded in 1961.[2]

This conflicts with the history of the village given here, which is supposed founded in the beginning of the Ming Dynasty. Unless it's part of the Foundation's cover story.

Or it could be a reference to China's lost history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. This place is real!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty good. It's not really over the top in any way, but it still manages to be interesting. +1

Fuck their ancestors to the eighteenth generation!

Also that part was really funny because I actually know what that is in Chinese.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't there already a footnote on what hukou is? Unless you want more elaboration beyond household registry…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, so it's a real building, that was my only problem with this so +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really quite good. :D A good anomaly, a good story, a good history lesson. I would appreciate a footnote on hukou to explain what that is. A cultural footnote for the significance of Huaxi would be useful, but not necessary; the savvy reader can always Google that for themselves. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except, it's not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder if a little camera blur would help sell the image. Everything in the shot is perfectly in focus, from the very top of the very tall building, which looks to be a few hundred meters away, to the treeline right in front of the camera.

[An option I threw together.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this very interesting however the first picture bothers me, it looks very CG.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its a living embodiment of the will of the people of Huaxi.
That's plenty anomalous on its own.

The references are also a strength of the article because they are local sentiments and history; exactly the sort of thing that would most powerfully affect this entity. (Besides, the only reference I wasn't already at least passingly familiar with was the term Hukuo. Give us 老外 a little credit. X3)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very cool; simple and elegant anomaly with deep ties to the lore of the place it hails from. I particularly like the interviews where you get to see glimpses of the "person" behind the living zeitgeist: their grief, anger, and regret at the times they weren't able to protect their people, the inner turmoil when they found themselves split between viewpoints (being driven to embrace the revolution while still tied equally to those who openly or secretly wanted to preserve the traditional ways…). Stellar writing, seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, that makes sense. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
实际上我自己都get不到点。我们群组里的人也大凡如是。没准可以改的更离谱一点。
In fact I can't get point even.And most has trouble fully understanding in Chinese group.Maybe it needs to have some real creepy anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's intentional on my part, since this SCP is very much tied to Chinese history. Readers who aren't familiar with the references can read the footnotes, which I think provide a pretty good explanation of what I'm referring to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
太多只有中国人才能明白的梗了。不跟中国人打交道的老外想看懂比较难。
So many Chinese local memetics. It is hard to understand if reader has never deal with Chinese.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a bit confused about his anomalous effect. If only those in the village can interact with him, what happens with those who don't live there? Does he just ignore them, or is it the other way around?

Since people who aren't from the village can't perceive him in the first place, they can't interact with him at all.

I also don't get why you often referred to him by his blacked out name most of the time instead of his SCP designation.

The first interview took place before the anomaly was given a designation. In the third interview, the designation wasn't used because the SCP was cooperative and Dr. Wang wanted to establish a friendly, conversational tone for the interview. Also, as people have pointed out to me in the past, there's also no particular need to reveal operational information (SCP designations) to SCPs or D-Classes.

I don't really get the sudden change in tone here. Is it because he copies the worldview of those in the village? That would make sense, but I'd still like to know.

Anti-Japanese sentiment is a pretty big deal in China. Also, considering that SCP-2788 saw hundreds of the people he was supposed to protect be murdered by the Japanese, it would surprise me if he wasn't angry at them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a bit confused about his anomalous effect. If only those in the village can interact with him, what happens with those who don't live there? Does he just ignore them, or is it the other way around?

I also don't get why you often referred to him by his blacked out name most of the time instead of his SCP designation.

The last interview kind of turned it off for me. I really dislike exposition dumps, but I can understand why it's there (because it's an interview, whoops).
In addition…

Fuck their ancestors to the eighteenth generation!

I don't really get the sudden change in tone here. Is it because he copies the worldview of those in the village? That would make sense, but I'd still like to know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They thought that it was some kind of magic, but in reality it was just sound agricultural advice.

My headcanon of it is that 2788 actually used magic and divine powers, but current anti-superstition/secular rhetoric forced it to change its lines according to the zeitgeist of the villagers.

item.title: Attributions
item.content:encoded:

"华西 Huaxi Tourism" by ImmerQi is licensed under CC BY-ND 2.0.
